coredata.dc:title: Carbonaceous nanomaterial-TiO2 heterojunctions for visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of aqueous organic pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yan
Hawboldt, Kelly
Zhang, Lijuan
Lu, Jie
Chang, Lantian
Dwyer, Anna
coredata.dc:description: Over the past few decades, heterogeneous photocatalysis has become a “green” alternative to traditional methods to degrade various environmental pollutants. TiO2, a low-cost and ultraviolet-light-active photocatalyst has been intensively used to decompose organic pollutants in water. The combination of TiO2 with non-toxic carbonaceous nanomaterials (CNMs) has been proved to extend TiO2 light absorption from ultraviolet (UV) to visible or even near infrared light region. Over the last decade, numerous studies on the development of CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions as efficient visible-light-responsive photocatalysts have been reported. However, a comprehensive review with respect to their synthesis, structural characteristics, photocatalytic activity enhancement is absent. In this review, the construction and characteristics (e.g. band structures, optical absorption, and interfacial charge transfer) of each class of CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions will be comprehensively reviewed. In addition, the application of CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions for photocatalytic degradation of persistent organic pollutants will be presented in detail. This review ends with a summary and future perspectives in exploring high performance CNM/TiO2 photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Carbonaceous nanomaterial-TiO2 heterojunctions for visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of aqueous organic pollutants

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Over the past few decades, heterogeneous photocatalysis has become a “green” alternative to traditional methods to degrade various environmental pollutants. TiO2, a low-cost and ultraviolet-light-active photocatalyst has been intensively used to decompose organic pollutants in water. The combination of TiO2 with non-toxic carbonaceous nanomaterials (CNMs) has been proved to extend TiO2 light absorption from ultraviolet (UV) to visible or even near infrared light region. Over the last decade, numerous studies on the development of CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions as efficient visible-light-responsive photocatalysts have been reported. However, a comprehensive review with respect to their synthesis, structural characteristics, photocatalytic activity enhancement is absent. In this review, the construction and characteristics (e.g. band structures, optical absorption, and interfacial charge transfer) of each class of CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions will be comprehensively reviewed. In addition, the application of CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions for photocatalytic degradation of persistent organic pollutants will be presented in detail. This review ends with a summary and future perspectives in exploring high performance CNM/TiO2 photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrialization and anthropogenic activities have accelerated water pollution by various organic contaminants (e.g. dyes, phenolic compounds, pesticides and pharmaceutical active compounds, etc.) [1,2]. These contaminants exhibit high toxicity, carcinogenicity and recalcitrant to degradation [3–7]. Over the last few decades, efforts have been devoted to developing environmentally sustainable and energy-efficient processes to remove or degrade organic contaminants from aqueous solutions. Photocatalysis, a process of accelerating a light-induced reaction in the presence of heterogeneous catalysts, can mitigate and/or eliminate water contamination through chemical conversion into harmless or less toxic components [8,9]. The development of visible-light-responsive photocatalysts is the main direction and objective of photocatalysis research.

Semiconductors, such as TiO2, ZnO, ZnS, CdS, CdTe, Fe2O3, MoS2 and WO3 are capable of conducting electricity at room temperature in the presence of light and hence have been extensively used as photocatalysts in various applications [10–16]. In a semiconductor, the energy difference between the bottom of the conduction band (CB) and the top of the valence band (VB) is defined as the band gap ( Fig. 1). When a photocatalyst is exposed to light of the desired wavelength (photon energy > band gap energy), the energy of photons is absorbed by electrons (e −) of VB that are excited to CB. This leaves behind unfilled VB, called holes (h +) [10]. The photogenerated electron–hole pairs break into free charge carriers, migrate to the photocatalyst surface, and participate in hole-mediated oxidation and electron-mediated reduction [17,18]. TiO2 is by far the most researched photocatalyst due to its low cost, high stability, nontoxicity and high reactivity [10]. However, TiO2 is an intrinsic wide band gap (3.07–3.2 eV) semiconductor and therefore limited by its absorption of UV light which only covers ~5% of the solar spectrum and the visible range of solar spectrum (~44%) cannot be utilized [19,20]. As such, it is essential to develop modification strategies of TiO2, which are capable of extending the range of light absorption.

Element doping [21–24] and heterostructure construction [25–29] are the major techniques for the development of visible-light-responsive TiO2-based photocatalysts. Doping TiO2 with metal/non-metal elements (B, N, S, Fe, etc.) extends its light absorption range and increases the photocatalytic activity due to the formation of new energy levels near the CB, which leads to narrowed band gap energy of the doped TiO2 photocatalysts. Heterostructure construction can directly enhance the absorption of visible light in TiO2 matrix by physically or chemically attaching a sensitizer that absorbs visible light [30]. Photosensitization involves the excitation of a sensitizer with narrower band gap, which transforms the light energy into chemical energy, and transfers it to TiO2 [31]. So far, organometallic, organic (dyes), and nanomaterial sensitizers are available for the heterostructure construction of TiO2 [31]. However, organometallic and organic sensitizers are either expensive or toxic, making them inappropriate to be used in environmental photocatalysis. It is essential to select low-cost and non-toxic sensitizers for heterostructure construction of TiO2.

Semiconducting carbonaceous nanomaterials (CNMs), including carbon quantum dots (CQDs, band gap: 2.47 eV), graphene quantum dots (GQDs, band gap: 1.8–2.4 eV), carbon nanotubes (CNTs; band gap: 0.5 eV), graphene oxide (GO, band gap: 2.2 eV), reduced graphene oxide (rGO, band gap: 1.15–2.0 eV), and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, band gap: 2.70 eV) are good candidates for sensitizers of TiO2 [32–36]. Compared with other types of sensitizers, CNMs have the advantages of low cost, low toxicity, tunable band gap energy and rapid electron transfer property. Photosensitization of TiO2 with CNMs leads to enhanced visible-light-harvesting [37]. Moreover, recombination of electron-hole pairs can also be significantly hindered because CNMs can act as electron sink to trap photogenerated electrons from TiO2 [38]. Although some CNMs suffer from the drawback of relatively lower energy conversion efficiency, CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions have been intensively studied for more than 20 years and have been proved to be efficient in improving the photocatalytic properties of TiO2. In 2011, Leary and Westwood summarized the synthesis routes, mechanisms of enhancement and applications of CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions, covering the conjugation of TiO2 with activated carbon, CNTs, fullerenes, graphene, and nanometric carbon black [39]. Khalid and co-workers provided another review on the design and synthesis of carbonaceous-TiO2 photocatalysts in 2017, covering carbon doping, activated carbon, fullerenes, CNTs and graphene [40]. Nonetheless, numerous studies on the conjugation of TiO2 with new CNMs (such as CQDs, GQDs, rGO, and g-C3N4) have been reported over the past decade. It is therefore important to review the most recent research development of CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions and their application to the visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of water contaminants.

This review is focused on the most recent progress (from 2012 to 2021) in the design, construction, and applications of visible-light-responsive CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions. As such, CNMs reviewed in this article are confined to those with energy band structures and being visible-light responsive. This review article begins with an overview of the fabrication methods and the enhancement in photocatalytic activity for each class of CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions, namely, carbon dots incorporated TiO2, CNT/TiO2, GO/TiO2 (or rGO/TiO2), and g-C3N4/TiO2. This is followed by the explanations on the photocatalytic degradation mechanisms of organic pollutants over CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions, and applications of CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions in photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes, phenolic compounds, and pharmaceutical and personal care products. This review will be concluded with a summary of and an outlook on the major challenges and perspectives for future research. For the first time, the roles of semiconducting CNMs in the heterojunctions for visible-light-driven photocatalytic reactions are summarized and explicated in this review. We hope this review will elucidate the synthesis, characterization, and ameliorated photocatalytic activity of CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions, and strengthen the efforts towards the development of cost-effective CNM/TiO2 photocalysts for environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and characteristics of CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions
ce:section.ce:para: CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions with different structures can been prepared via mixing [41], calcination [42], sol–gel process [43], hydro-/solvothermal synthesis [44] and deposition techniques [45]. Regardless of method, the optimization of synthetic conditions (such as CNM loading, solution pH, reaction temperature, reaction time, etc.) is essential for high-performance CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions with desirable charge carrier transfer in photocatalytic reactions. In this section, we will introduce specific synthetic routes and enhanced photocatalytic properties for recently reported CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions.

Carbon and graphene quantum dots (CQDs and GQDs) are zero-dimensional (0D) carbon-based quantum dots (hereinafter referred to as carbon dots) with fluorescence properties [46]. They have similar compositions (composed of C, O, H, N elements, etc.), but differ in morphology and structure. CQDs are amorphous quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with particle size less than 10 nm, whereas GQDs are segments of flaky graphene/graphite or other 3D graphitic materials with higher crystallinity [47–49]. Owing to their extremely small size, quantum confinement, edge effect, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and photostability, CQDs and GQDs are the most promising CNMs for applications in photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, bioimaging and sensing applications. Modification of TiO2 with CQDs/GQDs can effectively reduce the band gap energy, broaden the light absorption range and improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges of TiO2, leading to improved light energy harvesting and strong redox ability [50].

CQDs and GQDs can be prepared by either top-down or bottom-up method. The former involves cleaving or breaking down of carbonaceous materials via chemical, electrochemical, or physical approaches. The latter refers to the pyrolysis or carbonization of small organic molecules or by step-wise chemical fusion of small aromatic molecules. More specifically, CQDs can be prepared facilely by top-down (e.g., arc discharge [51], laser ablation [52], electrochemical method [53], etc.) or bottom-up (e.g. hydro-/solvothermal [54,55], microwave-assisted [56], and template synthesis [57]) from various carbon precursors. Whereas, GQDs are mainly prepared by top-down (such as electrochemical exfoliation, ultrasonication exfoliation, acidic oxidation, electron beam lithography, and chemical exfoliation) methods via incising the graphene sheet, CNTs, carbon fibers or graphite [58–62]. Sometimes, GQDs are also synthesized from polycyclic aromatics (such as pyrene, graphene oxide) through bottom-up (e.g., cage-opening of fullerene, microwave, and hydro-/solvothermal synthesis) methods [63–65]. It should be noted that surface properties (polarity, optical transmission or reflection, etc.) of CQDs and GQDs can be fine-tuned through heteroatom (N, B, P, S) doping or surface passivation [66–70]. CQDs/GQDs with tailored surface properties not only benefit the attainment of stable CQDs/TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 heterojunctions, but also improve the photoconversion efficiency of heterojunctions through the enhanced adsorption capacity and/or light-harvesting efficiency.

CQDs/TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 heterojunctions can be constructed via either one-pot (in situ) synthesis or multi-step hybridization route [47,71]. With one-pot synthesis ( Fig. 2), all the starting materials for the heterojunction composite are mixed together for hydro-/solvothermal treatment [72–74]. CQDs/TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 heterojunctions generated by one-pot synthesis are very stable and able to promote charge separation at their interfaces. More commonly, heterojunctions are prepared via multi-step hybridization process, in which the pre-made CQDs/GQDs are incorporated into the TiO2 matrix by either physical mixing or chemical bonding [47]. Physical mixing involves blending of CQDs/GQDs with TiO2 by stirring or ultrasonication and then combining them together through noncovalent interactions such as electrostatic adsorption, hydrogen-bond or π–π stacking interaction [75–80]. Although facile and rapid, the interaction between CQDs/GQDs and TiO2 by physical mixing is generally weak and unstable, limiting the charge separation and transfer at their interfaces [75]. The construction of CQDs/TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 heterojunctions by chemical bonding via hydrothermal deposition or calcination method [81–87] is mainly through the formation of a covalent (Ti–O–C) bond between TiO2 and –OH group on the CQDs/GQDs surface [47,82,85,88–92]. Compared with one-pot synthesis, multi-step hybridization provides more flexibility in tuning the surface properties of CQDs/GQDs, leading to the enhanced photocatalytic performance [71].

The resultant CQDs/TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 heterojunctions share the common features of narrow band gap [74,78,79,82,85,86,88,92,93], enhanced absorption of visible light [41,74,77–80,82,83,85–91,93], and prolonged lifetime of excited electrons and holes [74,82,86–89,91,93], which significantly improve the photocatalytic performance in degrading water contaminants. Three mechanisms have been proposed to explain the enhanced photocatalytic properties of CQDs/TiO2 and GQDs/TiO2 heterojunctions. Firstly, CQDs/GQDs have strong capabilities to directly absorb the visible or NIR light due to the presence of various functional groups (–COOH, –OH, –NH2, etc.) on the outer layer of CDQs/GQDs and the molecular fluorophores generated in most CQDs/GQDs synthetic protocols [94]. Hybridization of CQDs/GQDs with TiO2 extends the light response of TiO2 into the visible-light region of the solar spectrum, due to the electronic coupling between the π states of graphite-essential CQDs/GQDs and the CB states of TiO2 [32]. The electronic coupling will induce a smaller nano-hybrid band gap compared to pure TiO2, and bring about reduced band gap energy and a red-shift of the heterojunctions. In addition, CQDs/GQDs can also act as electron scavengers and improve the carrier separation by retarding the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes at the junction interface [32].

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs), consisting of graphitic sp2 carbons, are tubular (one-dimensional, 1D) CNM and have either metallic or semiconducting properties depending on their chirality [95–97]. They can be either single-walled (SWCNTs) with a diameter of 0.8–2 nm or multi-walled (MWCNTs) consisting of several concentrically interlinked SWCNTs, with diameters reaching more than 100 nm [98–100]. Several techniques including arc discharge [95,101], laser ablation [102,103], flame synthesis [104,105], and chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [106–108] have been developed to produce CNTs in sizable quantities. Owing to the good electronic transport and electron accepting properties, CNTs are available as both sensitizers that participate in light absorption, and electron reservoirs that capture the excited electrons of TiO2 [97,109–111].

CNTs can be coupled with TiO2 in different ways, leading to different morphologies of heterojunctions [39]. In general, three categories of CNTs/TiO2 heterostructures have been reported. In the first category, CNTs were deposited/grown on TiO2 nanoparticles ( Fig. 3a) [112–114] or TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNT) (Fig. 3b) [115,116]. In the second category, CNTs act as catalyst support [34,117] and TiO2 can be incorporated or coated on SWCNTs [118–120] or MWCNTs [111,121–125] to form TiO2 @CNT nanofibers (Fig. 3e). Lastly, intermixed CNTs and TiO2 heterojunctions were also prepared [126].

Many synthetic techniques have been employed to prepare CNTs/TiO2 heterojunctions [39,127]. The most published configuration, TiO2 nanoparticles bound to the surface of CNTs, is usually achieved via the sol–gel or hydrothermal method by nucleating and growing TiO2 on CNTs dispersed in a liquid medium [118–124,128–130]. Normally, the composite produced with the sol–gel method has more homogenously dispersed TiO2 nanoparticles on the surface of CNTs, while the hydrothermal method features compact aggregation of larger TiO2 nanoparticles loaded on the bundles or networks of CNTs [127]. Electrospinning, after sol–gel and hydrothermal methods, is also used to fabricate TiO2/CNT nanofibers [131,132]. Well-dispersed but mutually connected nanofibers with a uniformly distributed diameter were fabricated and the surfaces of nanofibers was covered with densely distributed TiO2 nanoparticles. Less commonly, electrophoretic deposition [133], atomic layer deposition [134], CVD [116], and sputter deposition [135] were employed to construct TiO2 on CNT (Category 2) heterojunctions. Meanwhile, hydrothermal method and CVD were used to synthesize CNTs on TiO2 (Category 1) heterojunctions [39,113].

CNTs contribute to the improved photocatalytic activity of CNT/TiO2 heterojunctions via three routes [39]. The first enhancement lies in the increased adsorption of organic pollutants on the surface of heterojunctions due to the large specific surface area of CNTs, typically 400–900 m2 g−1 for SWCNTs and 50–500 m2 g−1 for MWCNTs [39,99,123,136], which provide high reactive surface area and high-density of active sites for photocatalytic reactions. CNTs are reported to synergistically enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 through the retardation of electron–hole recombination [39,110,113–116,118,120,121,126,127,136–139]. As outlined by Woan et al. [109], CNTs, serving as an electron sink ( Fig. 4 .), are capable of forming a barrier junction at the CNT/TiO2 interface, which offers an effective route of reducing electron-hole recombination by improving the injection of electrons into the CNTs and preventing the transfer of holes from TiO2 to CNTs [109]. Additionally, CNTs (as a sensitizer) are responsible for extending the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 into the visible and natural sunlight region [39,113,116,118,120,121,126–128,137,140]. In this scheme (Fig. 4), the photogenerated electrons in the CNTs are transferred to the CB of the TiO2, permitting a reduction process. The positively charged CNTs then trap electrons from the VB of TiO2, leaving holes. The positively charged TiO2 can thus take part in an oxidation process.

The high-cost related with the production of high purity and structured-defined CNTs limits their applications [141,142] in spite of the advances in preferential synthesis of SWCNTs with selectivity of 90–95% [143,144]. Production of pure semiconducting SWCNTs remains cost-prohibitive [141]. Similarly, MWCNTs can be produced in high volume through large-scale CVP, however they suffer from structural deviations, contaminations, and impurities [141,145].

Graphene and graphene oxide (GO) have also been reported to increase the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 [39,146]. Graphene is a two-dimensional (2D) CNM made of a basal monolayer of sp2–hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb lattice [147,148]. Unlike other carbon nanoallotropes, graphene is a zero band gap semiconductor (or semimetal) with the VB and CB contacting at the Brillouin zone corners [149]. The zero band gap property make the pristine form of graphene inappropriate to modify TiO2 for photocatalysis [146,149]. Nonetheless, GO and reduced GO (rGO), oxidized forms of graphene have excellent semiconducting properties and can play a role similar to CNTs when they form heterojunctions with TiO2. In particular, chemically exfoliated GO/rGO can be produced rapidly at low cost [147,150]. As such, this review will focus on the GO/TiO2 and rGO/TiO2 heterojunctions.

Generally, GO/rGO hybridized TiO2 can be fabricated via either ex situ hybridization, or in situ crystallization [151]. The ex situ hybridization involves the mixing of GO/rGO nanosheets and pre-synthesized or commercially available TiO2 in solutions [152–161]. Before mixing, it is common to modify surface of TiO2 and/or GO/rGO sheets, so that they can bind through either the non-covalent (π–π interaction, hydrogen bond) interactions or chemical bonding [152,158,159]. In ex situ hybridization, nanostructures with desired functionalities can be pre-selected, however there is potential for low density and non-uniform coverage of TiO2 on the GO/rGO surfaces [151,155]. In contrast, in situ crystallization, which involves the formation of TiO2 directly on GO surface using oxygen-containing groups of GO as nucleation sites, allows a uniform surface coverage of TiO2 nanoparticles [150,151]. The widely used in situ crystallization techniques include hydro/solvothermal [162–166], sol–gel [167,168], redox reaction [169,170], and self-assembly [171,172]. Hydro-/solvothermal method is a simple and efficient way to prepare high quality heterojunctions at a large scale [173]. For example, Shen et al., synthesized the GO-supported TiO2 using an in situ hydrothermal process, in which crystallization of TiO2 nanocrystals from TiCl4 solution took place on GO surface [166]. Hydro-/solvothermal methods offer the additional advantage of partially or completely reducing GO to rGO during the crystallization process [174]. The construction of GO/TiO2 heterojunctions by the hydro-/solvothermal method depends on numerous factors, including the source of metal, temperature, pH, solvent, and time, which must be optimized. Sol-gel synthesis is a low cost and low temperature method, in which TiO2 precursors (such as TiCl3, C12H28O4Ti, and Ti(C4H9O)4) undergo a series of hydrolysis and polycondensation reactions [151,168]. The key advantage of the in situ sol–gel process lies in the fact that the surface OH– groups of the GO/rGO sheets act as the nucleation sites for the hydrolysis, so that the resulting TiO2 nanostructures are chemically bonded to the GO/rGO surfaces. GO/TiO2 composite was also prepared via in situ redox reaction [169]. This reaction allowed the simultaneous reduction of GO and subsequent oxidation of TiCl3 and SnCl2 precursors to SnO2 and TiO2 on the rGO sheet. Recently, a novel in situ self-assembly method ( Fig. 5) was developed to prepare an ordered TiO2/GO composite [151,171]. In this method, Pluronic P123 served as both carbon precursor and structure-directing agent, which assembled at the water/oil interfaces and subsequently was carbonized in situ to a GO sheet where TiO2 grew gradually along the preferred direction [171]. Each of the aforementioned processes has pros and cons, and therefore process selection normally depends on the desired properties of heterojunctions, availability of instrumentation, and specific applications intended for the photocatalysts [150].

Similar to CNT/TiO2, three mechanisms contribute to the enhancement in photocatalytic activity of GO/TiO2 and rGO/TiO2 heterojunctions [39,146,150,151]. The first mechanism is the increased adsorption of the organic pollutants on the surface of GO/rGO sheets. Aromatic organic pollutant adsorption on GO/rGO is promoted by π–π stacking interactions between the sp2 domains from both systems [151,169]. The second mechanism is related to light absorption. The band gap of the heterojunctions is narrowed after attachment of TiO2 to GO/rGO surfaces, leading to a red shift in light absorption and better utilization of solar energy [169]. The third mechanism is related to the charge carrier separation and transport. Electron transfer rate can be improved when TiO2 nanoparticles are anchored on GO/rGO sheets [169,175]. The covalent chemical bonding formed at the interface between TiO2 and GO/rGO could facilitate the charge transfer from TiO2 to GO/rGO and hinder the electron-hole recombination.

Polymeric graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a relatively new CNM introduced to the area of photocatalysis [176,177]. g-C3N4 exhibits 2D layered structure and is often regarded as sp2-hybridized nitrogen-substituted graphene [178,179]. The moderate band gap (~2.7 eV) and suitable CB (−1.3 eV) and VB (1.4 eV) edge positions associated with g-C3N4 make for efficient visible-light absorption and high reduction-oxidation capacities [176,180,181]. Moreover, g-C3N4 could be synthesized readily by thermal polymerization from low cost available precursors [182,183]. It has therefore attracted enormous attention as catalysts or co-catalysts in green technologies for energy conversion and storage [183–185], fuel cells [186], and environmental remediation [180,182,187,188]. Nonetheless, the photocatalytic activity of pure g-C3N4 is quite low due to its high charge carrier recombination, low absorption coefficient, and low specific surface area [180,181]. Combining g-C3N4 with traditional wide band gap semiconductors to form g-C3N4–based heterojunctions is proposed as an effective method to enhance its photocatalytic performance [184,186,189]. The synthesis and properties of g-C3N4/TiO2 heterojunctions are discussed below.

Analogous to GO/TiO2 heterojunctions, g-C3N4/TiO2 heterojunctions can be prepared by ex situ hybridization or in situ synthesis [182,190,191]. The ex situ hybridization via solution mixing [190,192–194], calcination [195–197], hydro-/solvothermal [198,199] and sol-gel [200] are widely reported. In situ synthesis of g-C3N4/TiO2 heterojunctions via calcination [201–204], hydro-/solvothermal [205–210] and pyrolysis [211] has also been developed. Li et al. recently prepared TiO2 @g-C3N4 heterojunctions by an in situ seed-induced solvothermal method [210]. SEM/EDS and TEM results showed that mesoporous TiO2 nanospheres were formed and grew on the surface of seeded g-C3N4 by self-assembled anatase nanocrystals. The seed concentration played a key role in tuning the dispersion and particle size of the newly-grown TiO2 ( Fig. 6). The dispersion and the particle size of TiO2 was improved by the increased seed concentration over the range of 30–60 μM, and well grown and good distribution of TiO2 on g-C3N4 nanosheets were observed (Fig. 6a–e). Further increasing the seed concentration inhibited the self-assembly of TiO2 nanospheres, leading to complete suppression of the formation of TiO2 nanospheres after 1.0 mM of seeding (Fig. 6g–h).

According to the charge-transfer mechanisms, g-C3N4 and TiO2 will form a type II heterojunction photocatalyst ( Fig. 7a), the preferred junction type, which is featured by staggered band edge potentials between g-C3N4 and TiO2 [182,191,212]. Once exposed to visible light, photogenerated electrons move from CB of g-C3N4 to CB of TiO2 and holes transfer from VB of TiO2 to VB of g-C3N4 [212]. The migration of charge carriers in the opposite direction significantly enhances the electron−hole spatial separation and retards the charge recombination to prolong the lifetime of free electrons and holes [182].

Recently, ternary CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions with superior photocatalytic activity have been developed for various applications [92,213–218]. Most of the ternary heterojunctions are 0D and 2D CNM co-sensitized TiO2 with the combined merits of large surface area, extended light absorption range, and prolonged lifetime of free electrons and holes [213]. Ternary layered CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanosheets were prepared by impregnation precipitation method and were used as a photocatalyst for H2 production [215]. The hydrogen evolution rate by the ternary composite was about 5 times of that of the binary g-C3N/TiO2 composite. Results from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and lifetime of photoinduced carriers confirmed that addition of g-C3N4 to the composite helps reduce the impedance of the photocatalyst. The CQDs mainly serve as electron reservoir, capable of reducing the recombination of the photogenerated carriers [215]. In another work, CQDs decorated TiO2/g-C3N4 film was used as a photoanode for photoelectrocatalytic degradation of 1,4-dioxane [217]. Compared with TiO2 and TiO2/g-C3N4 nanorod arrays, a much narrower band gap (2.47 eV) and longer lifetime of photoinduced electron-hole pairs were observed in the ternary CQDs/TiO2/g-C3N4 heterojunction, leading to a 77.9% degradation rate of 1,4-dioxane, 50% higher than that with the binary TiO2/g-C3N4 electrode. The improved degradation efficiency achieved by the ternary photoanode is attributed to the formation of a Z-scheme heterojunction (Fig. 7b) with enhanced redox capacities [182,217].

As reported in multiple research studies, the CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions work as a cascade photosystem of outside-in light harvesting and inside-out directed transferring of charge [92,182]. The photoconversion efficiency highly depends on the on the cumulative efficiency of the cascade effect of the photogenerated carriers. The oriented transfer of energy could be the most efficient way to utilize the solar (or visible) energy in photocatalytic heterojunctions [92]. To construct directed charge-transfer cascades/heterojunctions, a spatial distribution of titanium-defected TiO2@CQDs@rGO (V Ti@CQDs@rGO) with a core−shell structure was synthesized by Lu and co-workers using one-pot hydrothermal method [92]. The V Ti@CQDs@rGO heterojunction demonstrated an order of magnitude enhancement of the photoenergy utilization compared to the random surface heretojunction of TiO2/CQDs/rGO [92]. Results from time-resolved photoluminescence, UV-Vis DRS and XPS valence band spectra proved that spatially inside-out electron transfer and the charge collection effect on the surface play a key role in promoting the hole transfer of the ternary heterojunction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms for photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants by CNM/TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants by CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions is a process in which visible, or even IR light is utilized to transform an environmental pollutant into less harmful products or degrade completely. Based on the reported band edge (CB and VB) positions of CNMs and TiO2, most of the CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions belong to type II heterojunction (Fig. 7a), in which electrons flow towards the less negative potential and holes flow to the less positive potential [77,180,211,219–221]. Under visible light irradiation, photo-excited electrons are generated in the CNMs and electron transfer takes place from the CB of CNMs to the CB of TiO2 while the migration of holes occurs in the opposite direction. This spatially separates the photo-excited charge carriers and reduce potential for recombination [212]. However, the migration of the photogenerated electrons to the less negative CB and holes to the less positive VB reduces the redox abilities of electrons and holes in type II heterojunction [182,222,223]. To solve this problem, a novel Z-scheme heterojunction (Fig. 7b) of CNM/TiO2 was recently developed [192,224]. The Z-scheme heterojunction has the same band alignment as that of type II heterojunction, but differs in the charge transfer mechanism. Due to different CB and VB edges between CNMs and TiO2, photo-induced electrons in a Z-scheme heterojunction are transferred from the CB of TiO2 to the VB of CNMs. This results in oxidation on the surface with higher oxidation potential (TiO2 surface) and reduction on the surface with higher reduction potential (CNM surface) [225]. Furthermore, the charge migration in the Z-scheme heterojunction is based on the electrostatic attraction between electrons and holes, which is easier than that of type II heterojunction [182]. Research on the development of Z-scheme heterojunction of CNM/TiO2 is still in the early stages, further research efforts are required.

The mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants involves a chain of oxidative and reductive reactions on the surface of CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions ( Fig. 8). The photo-generated electron/hole pairs participate in the oxidation-reduction reactions with adsorbed H2O and O2 on the photocatalyst surface, generating reactive oxygen species, viz. hydroxyl (•OH) and superoxide (
O
2
• −
) radicals [182,220,226–228]. In parallel, the photogenerated holes (h +) also react with surface OH− groups in aqueous systems to give •OH radicals [36,209,229]. It is believed that the reaction of •OH radicals with organic pollutants leads to the decomposition of these compounds [182,230]. The generation of radicals and the interaction between the formed radicals with organic pollutants can be explained in the following procedures and equations:
•

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of aqueous organic pollutants by CNM/TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Extensive research has been carried out on visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants (dyes, fertilizers, pesticides, pharmaceutical ingredients, phenols and organohalides, etc.) from water using CNM/TiO2. This work is outlined below, categorized according to target contaminant(s).

Organic dyes are widely used in textile, cosmetic, paper, food, agriculture and pharmaceutical industries. Organic dyes discharged to water bodies produces undesirable color and restricts the penetration of sunlight which can lead to a variety of negative environmental impacts such as diminished photosynthetic activity of algae, death of aquatic life, and poisoning of surface/ground water [231]. The complexity and variability of the chemical structures of organic dyes makes them difficult to remove using traditional processes and typically requires multiple treatments [232,233]. Photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes using CNM/TiO2 has been proved to be an efficient removal method from wastewater [41,44,80,128,140,163,196].

CQDs and GQDs loaded TiO2 heterojunctions have tested for degradation of various organic dyes, e.g. methylene blue (MB), methyl orange (MO), rhodamine B (RhB), and acid red 88 (AR88) [41,44,73,74,77,80,81,90,234–243]. For example, CQDs/TiO2 heterojunctions prepared by energy-efficient impregnation method with different CQDs loadings were used for photocatalytic degradation of RhB (10.0 mg/L) under visible irradiation [74]. The band gap energies of the prepared CQDs/TiO2 heterojunctions were much narrower than that of pure TiO2 and decreased with the increased amount of CQDs ( Fig. 9a–b), leading to the enhanced visible light absorption. The composite with the optimum CQDs content (6.0 wt%) was capable of degrading 93.4% of RhB in 4 h (Fig. 9c), with a degradation rate constant 42.7 times faster than that of pure TiO2 [74]. Good chemical stability was also observed from the prepared composites, there is no significant decrease in the photocatalytic degradation efficiency after 4 cycles of reuse as seen from Fig. 9d [74]. A similar study in which N-doped GQDs and TiO2 (N-GQDs/TiO2) heterojunctions were fabricated to degrade MO (10.0 mg/L) under visible light irradiation [44]. Experimental results indicated that the composite containing 1.0 wt% of N-GQDs provided the highest MO degradation efficiency (~95.0%) in 2 h, a threefold enhancement in the photocatalytic activity compared with pure TiO2 under the same conditions [44]. Other applications of CQDs or GQDs-incorporated TiO2 heterojunctions for photodegradation of organic dyes are listed in Table 1.

CNT/TiO2 heterojunctions also demonstrates enhanced photodegradation efficiency of organic dyes under visible light irradiation [110,128,140,244]. Chinonso and co-workers loaded TiO2 onto MWCNT using the hydrothermal method followed by air plasma treatment for photocatalytic degradation of MB-1 and MB-2 under visible light irradiation [110]. Plasma treatment was used to improve the hydrophilicity of CNT and interfacial adhesion to TiO2. As seen from Fig. 10a–b, the composite without plasma treatment had a 3D structure with very rough surface, whereas the composite after plasma treatment had a finer and flatter surface with TiO2 being uniformly distributed in the CNT network. Incorporating CNT to TiO2 reduced the band gap energy for the prepared composite (Fig. 10c), resulting in enhanced photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation (Fig. 10d) [110]. The CNT/TiO2 heterojunctions fabricated by in situ growth of TiO2 on the surface of CNT were used as photocatalysts to degrade RhB (10.0 mg/L) under simulated sunlight [140]. The two semiconductors (CNT and TiO2) were found to form a n-n type heterojunction, which could effectively promote the transport and migration of photo-generated carriers. In addition, CNT/TiO2 heterojunctions had much larger specific surface area (129.2 m2/g) than pure TiO2 (33.2 m2/g), which increased the contact area and adsorption capacity of RhB. Results indicated that the composite with 10 wt% of CNT calcined at 450 °C showed the best photodegradation efficiency, being able to completely degrade RhB within 25 min [140].

GO/TiO2 and rGO/TiO2 heterojunctions showed significant enhancement in the photodegradation of organic dyes relative to TiO2 [163,172,245–248]. Photocatalytic degradation of MO over rGO/TiO2 heterojunctions (prepared by liquid phase deposition followed by thermal treatment) was investigated by Pastrana-Martínez et al. [245]. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of MO under visible light irradiation (λ > 430 nm) was evaluated as a function of the rGO content and thermal treatment temperature of the prepared heterojunctions. Almost complete degradation and mineralization of MO (10.0 mg/L) was achieved in less than 60 min over the optimum rGO/TiO2 composite (thermally treatment at 200 °C with 3.3 wt% of rGO) [245]. In a related study, rGO/TiO2 heterojunctions with different TiO2/rGO mass ratios were prepared via the hydrothermal method [247]. The composite with the TiO2/rGO mass ratio of 0.5 provided the highest and most rapid degradation rate of MB (20.0 mg/L) under visible light (λ > 400 nm) due to the extremely high specific adsorption area (124.0 m2/g, pore size of 6.05 nm) and the optimal dosage of rGO in the composite [247].

As discussed in Section 3, g-C3N4/TiO2 heterojunctions are also effective visible-light-responsive photocatalysts for degradation of dyes from water [193,196,198,210,249–252]. Lei et al. used a simple vacuum calcination method to synthesize g-C3N4/TiO2 that showed very good photocatalytic performance for the degradation of azo dye acid orange 7 (AO7, 20.0 mg/L) under solar light irradiation [250]. The UV–visible spectra of the bare g-C3N4, TiO2, and g-C3N4/TiO2 heterojunctions clearly show that the red shift of absorption edge from 380 nm to 460 nm occurred as the amount of g-C3N4 in the composite increased. The g-C3N4/TiO2 heterojunctions demonstrate excellent degradation efficiency (99.0%) of AO7 after 3 h of visible light irradiation [250]. In a recent study [193], g-C3N4/TiO2 heterojunctions with different morphologies (nanorod, nanosphere and nanotube) were constructed and were used as photocatalysts to degrade RhB (5.0 mg/L) under visible light irradiation. The best degradation performance was obtained with a g-C3N4/TiO2 nanotube, which demonstrated 97.0% degradation of RhB within 80 min. Degradation efficiencies of RhB by g-C3N4/TiO2 nanosphere and nanorod were 92.0% and 94.5% respectively, also much higher than that (74.0%) by commercial TiO2 [193].

All these aforementioned examples confirm that CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions are efficient for photodegradation of different dye molecules under solar or visible light irradiation. Table 1 summarizes the reported studies on the photodegradation of organic dyes by CNM/TiO2 after 2011.

Phenol and phenolic compounds can be recalcitrant and toxic. Sources of phenols to watershed include wastes from paper manufacturing, agriculture, pharmaceuticals, petrochemical industry, coal processing, or municipal wastes [253]. Phenolic compounds act as carcinogens and damage red blood cells and the liver even at low concentrations [254]. Complete removal of phenolic compounds from aquatic environment is a mandatory requirement to safeguard life of humans and aquatic organisms against possible contamination of these toxic chemicals. Compared with physical removal methods, chemical methods that are able to decompose the toxic compounds are preferred. Visible-light-driven photocatalysis of phenols is potentially more sustainable and cost-effective method for removal of phenolic compounds from water [253]. CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions have been studied for photocatalytic degradation of several phenolic compounds over the past decade. In this review, the use of CNM/TiO2 in photodegradation of phenolic compounds in terms of the specific catalyst and its synthetic method, type of phenol and initial concentration, catalyst dosage, the reaction conditions, degradation efficiency and performance enhancement relative to TiO2 are summarized in Table 2.

Carbon dots (CQDs and GQDs) sensitized TiO2 nanocomposites have shown good photodegradation performance of phenols. GQDs/TiO2 heterojunctions prepared by one-step sol-gel method were tested for the photodegradation of nonylphenol ethoxylate (NPE10O) under visible light radiation [256]. A composite of 20:1 TiO2/GQDs mass ratio demonstrated the best degradation efficiency, capable of completely degrading NPE10O (50.0 mg/L) with a catalyst dosage of 1.0 g/L within 60 min. Moreover, by detecting the degradation intermediates using the time-of-flight mass spectroscopy (TOF/MS), •OH,
O
2
• −
and h + have been proved to be involved in NPE10O degradation, and •OH attack was the dominant mechanism [256].

Photocatalytic degradation of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) by TiO2 @CNTs under NIR radiation (λ > 800 nm) was carried out by Yang et al. [114]. In this work, TiO2 microspheres were connected to MWCNTs that tightly enveloped the hierarchical TiO2 microspheres to build a stable heterogeneous photocatalyst. Large specific surface areas, 145.2 m2g−1 for TiO2 @CNTs(3%), and 189.9 m2g−1 for TiO2@CNTs(5%) were obtained for the as-prepared heterojunctions. Moreover, separation of electron-hole pairs was promoted and much higher current density and reduced resistance to photogenerated electron transfer were observed. The rate of decomposition of 4-NP (10.0 mg/L) was accelerated and the removal efficiency improved from 60% using TiO2 to 96.0% by TiO2 @CNTs(5%). Degradation efficiency varied from 100% at 5 mg L–1 of 4-NP, to 96% at 10 mg L–1 and 72.0% at 20 mg L–1. In addition, the heterojunction photocatalyst of TiO2 @CNTs(5%) showed very good chemical stability with a decrease in degradation efficiency from 100% to 90% after 5 cycles of photodegradation.

Pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) consist primarily of polar molecules with a wide variation in physicochemical properties [261–263]. PPCPs are transported to the aqueous environment through domestic wastewater, industrial wastewater, runoff and landfill leachate [264–266]. Typically, PPCPs are recalcitrant to degradation and persistent in the environment. Traditional wastewater treatment processes have limited success in removing or treating these compounds, potentially resulting in the formation of antibiotic resistant bacteria and genes [84,261]. As such, there is an urgent need to decompose PPCPs before they enter waterways. Visible light photodegradation has promising potential in this area. Table 3 summarizes recent studies on the photodegrdation of PPCPs by CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions under solar/visible irradiations.

Table 3 clearly indicate that GO and g-C3N4 sensitized TiO2 photocatalysts are efficient in degrading several commonly used antibiotics and medicines under solar/visible irradiation. A visible active rGO/TiO2 composite was synthesized via hydrothermal method and was used to degrade ciprofloxacin (CIP) irradiated with visible light [269]. Experimental results showed that 96.2% of CIP were decomposed after 60 min of irradiation in the presence of GO/TiO2 and 79.7% of CIP were decomposed using commercial TiO2 (P25). The higher photocatalytic activity of rGO/TiO2 was mainly due to the increase in visible light absorption, resulted from its lowered band gap energy (2.7 eV). To determine the active radical species for photodegradation, four radical scavengers, namely, triethanolamine (TEOA) for h +, benzoquinone for e –, isopropyl alcohol (IPA) for •OH, and ethanol for
O
2
• −
were used during the photodegradation process of CIP. Experimental results revealed that degradation efficiency reduced to 33% and 48.3% respectively by the addition of TEOA and IPA, indicating that h + and •OH made important contributions to the decomposition of CIP.

Most recently, Ning and co-workers used in situ thermal polymerization and hydrogenation treatment to fabricate surface defect structures of g-C3N4/TiO2 (H-CN/TiO2) heterojunctions for the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TTC-HCl) [273]. Through the hydrogenation treatment, surface defect structures (two coordinated nitrogen vacancies on g-C3N4 and oxygen vacancies on TiO2) were formed on the H-CN/TiO2 photocatalyst. TEM images of CN/TiO2 (without hydrogenation treatment) and H-CN/TiO2 in Fig. 11a–b revealed that the hydrogenation process did not generate a significant morphology change except that the surface of TiO2 became disordered (Fig. 11 c). By comparing the XPS spectra of N 1s and O 1s (Fig. 11 c–d) for CN/TiO2 and H-CN/TiO2 heterojunctions, it is observed that the concentration of N2C vacancies on the surface of g-C3N4 and oxygen vacancies on the surface of TiO2 were increased after hydrogenation. Results of transient photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of the two heterojunctions further confirmed that defect structures of H-CN/TiO2 provided more efficient separation and migration of photogenerated electron–hole pairs, leading to enhanced photodegradation efficiency of TTC-HCl (30.0 mg/L) (Fig. 11f–g) under visible light irradiation [273].

Although CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions demonstrate satisfactory degradation efficiency of PPCPs in the lab, their photoconversion efficiency needs to be further improved. This is demonstrated by the low E factor values obtained for some CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions (Table 3). Construction of CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions with ordered structures, which enable the oriented transfer of energy could be a solution to this problem. Design and construction of direct Z-Scheme CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions may also help in gaining high photoconversion efficiency.

Many research studies over the past two decades have confirmed that carbonaceous nanomaterial (CNM)-conjugated TiO2 heterojunctions with different nanostructures are promising materials for visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants. The enhancement of the photocatalytic performance of CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions relative to pristine TiO2 is attributed to (1) extension the absorption toward the visible-light region (visible-light harvesting), (2) limiting the recombination of photoinduced charge carriers, and (3) increasing the adsorption capacity of the organic pollutants on the photocatalyst surface.

Despite performance enhancement, numerous opportunities and challenges still exist for environmental photocatalysis. Firstly, many reported CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions still suffer from relatively low efficiency and low stability for repeated usage required by industry. Synthetic methods to further improve the photocatalytic capacity and stability of visible-light-responsive CNM/TiO2 photocatalysts are required. Secondly, synergistic effects among CNMs and TiO2 of the complex (e.g. ternary CNM/TiO2) heterojunctions should be studied in more detail. A profound understanding of charge transfer pathways is needed and be verified by associated characterization methods. Moreover, it is challenging to control the spatial structure of the CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions, and the well-contacted heterojunction interface between CNMs and TiO2 is difficult to achieve. This results in the relative low photoconversion efficiency of the CNM/TiO2 heterojunctions. As such, rational design of the nanostructures for the visible-light- responsive CNM/TiO2 photocatalysts should be given more emphasis to attain a high quantum efficiency. Lastly, theoretical calculations can be introduced into the development of the visible-light- responsive CNM/TiO2 photocatalysts and help to predict the degradation pathways as well as band structure changes of CNMs after being coupled with TiO2.
